title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
author: Stephen R. Covey

chapters.title: The right habits are the secret to success in life
chapters.text:
There are so many things that jostle for our attention. Your phone buzzes. Slack notifications pop up. Emails flood in. Your colleague stops by "just for a minute." Before you know it, your entire day has been hijacked by other people's priorities. If you don’t take care, you will sacrifice the things that matter for items that are not important but appear to need urgent attention. Time-management strategies that many of us apply often make us prioritize our existing schedule. This approach breeds efficiency rather than effectiveness.

Spend your time on achieving priorities rather than scheduling them.

Based on urgency and importance, all your activities fall into four categories:

• Urgent, important.
• Important, not urgent.
• Urgent, not important.
• Neither important nor urgent.

The most important is the second category. It contains things that determine how your life turns out in the end. Working in this direction, you find that the urgent things in your life decrease. However, you need to identify activities that fall into this category first. Then, learn to allocate time to them intentionally.
Important things that are not urgent might include: exercising regularly, planning your week, having meaningful conversations with family, learning new skills, or building key relationships. They never scream for attention, but they create the foundation for everything else.

Interactions with other people manifest our interconnectedness. In most situations, we try to have the edge over others when dealing with them. We think that someone else has to lose for us to win, but this is an incorrect paradigm because two people with a win-lose approach might end up with a lose-lose outcome.

There is usually enough for everyone’s needs, but not for everyone’s greed. When we think win-win, we create positive relationships that will be highly rewarding in the long run. So, make sure everyone leaves the negotiating table satisfied, even if the outcome is no deal. As long as the conditions satisfy both sides, there is no need to manipulate or pressure the other person. If that’s the case, it’s a lose-lose situation for both; after all, not everyone feels great after bearing someone down.

To have a win-win mentality, it’s vital to become sensitive and patient. These qualities will create an atmosphere of mutual trust that’s beneficial to everyone. More than this, we can apply a win-win mentality to our relationships beyond business transactions. For example, what can we do to achieve a win-win situation in relationships with our spouses, children, parents, or colleagues? Talk to them about trying out the new agreement. Take action.

chapters.title: Focus on specific areas in your life
chapters.text:
You need to pay attention to seven key areas to become highly effective. That means you must learn to:

1. Act, not react.
2. Predict consequences before pursuing the endeavor.
3. Schedule your priorities instead of prioritizing your schedule.
4. Seek the common benefits.
5. Listen to others before trying to be heard.
6. Work with others to achieve exponential results.
7. Keep an effective system running.

Once you’ve focused on these areas, you can start exploring your paradigms. Paradigms are the filter through which we see the world around us. Two people might look at a crowd and see entirely different things. When your worldview aligns with the basic principles upon which the universe operates, you can navigate life successfully.

For instance, a person with a negative paradigm will continually see negativity, while a person with a positive one will find comfort even in pain. Picture this: Two managers walk into the same meeting. One sees opportunity and potential in the team, while the other always looks for obstacles, problems, and flaws. Same meeting, completely different realities.

A paradigm is a pattern we apply to explain certain phenomena.

Moreover, our set of paradigms determines our character. Paradigms can spell the difference between success and failure in business and relationships. A pattern shift occurs when we question our set behaviors and attitudes and gradually change them.

For example, Stephen Covey experienced a paradigm shift one Sunday morning in a relatively silent subway car. A man and his children boarded, and the vehicle became noisy immediately. Surprisingly, the man did nothing to control his "undisciplined children." Stephen Covey became so irritated by the noise that he asked the father to supervise them. The man said they were coming from the hospital where their mother had died. He didn't want to control them because they were already distressed. His answer switched Stephen Covey's mood from irritation and annoyance to compassion and willingness to help. That is the power of the paradigm.

However, paradigm shifts are not usually sudden. They require a deliberate attempt to shift from the way we perceive things to developing qualities universally recognized as charitable.

Our paradigms, correct or incorrect, are the sources of our attitudes and behaviors, and ultimately our relationships with others. ~ Stephen R. Covey

chapters.title: Focus on proactivity rather than reaction
chapters.text:
Being proactive distinguishes us from other animals who respond to external stimuli. Animals react differently based on their genetic programming. They cannot analyze incentives. For instance, a dog gets kicked and bites back — it’s pure instinct. But you? You have a superpower called the pause. Between what happens to you and how you respond, there's a space. In that space lies your freedom. Unfortunately, not all of us use this human advantage, which is what we need to learn to fix.

Being proactive is taking responsibility for your actions instead of blaming circumstances.

Reactive people have their behavior and emotions dictated by external circumstances and feelings. When you hail a taxi, and someone else jumps in, you blame them. Ever notice how some people let a traffic jam ruin their entire day? Their mood depends on the weather, their boss's attitude, or their phone’s battery life. That's reactive living. These reactive people also fail to take responsibility when things don't go as planned.

In contrast to this, proactive people believe their mood or behavior is a function of their internal engineering. Rather than blame others for failure, they seek the way forward. They view life in terms of two concentric circles:

• Circle of concern
• Circle of influence

The first circle relates to things we worry about: the bills we need to pay, the loans we need to refund, the weather conditions, changing economic status, etc. The second one, the circle of influence, contains things we can change. Working in this area makes it expand when you recognize the things you can change. Yet, the circle shrinks when you focus on something you have little or no control over.

Proactivity makes you a highly competent person in the most extenuating circumstances. Austrian neurologist Victor Frankl was repeatedly imprisoned in German concentration camps during World War II. Despite the torture and deplorable conditions, he chose to respond rather than react to his circumstances. His whole focus was on his mind since that was the only thing Frankl could control. He thought of a happier life and how he would narrate his experiences in the concentration camps to others. Frankl used the small gap between external stimuli and action to find his freedom.

To start, challenge yourself to be proactive for 30 days. Choose to take responsibility when you feel tempted to blame others for your troubles. Remember that the real issue is not the problem itself but how you react to it.

chapters.title: Create precise mental pictures and flawless mission statements
chapters.text:
Everything gets created twice. First in your mind, then in reality. Right now, you're probably sitting in a building that someone imagined before the first brick was laid. Your smartphone existed in someone's mind before it existed in your hand. Therefore, to win, you must visualize what winning means and then implement it.

Proper planning is the key to the impeccable execution of any worthwhile goal.

Try to create time specifically for visualization. Write down your end goals and itemize the steps you would take to reach them. Before you begin, look at the end. Anticipate obstacles and plan solutions ahead of time. You won’t lose your way if you ask for directions repeatedly. Don’t rush. Instead, plan, prepare, and execute.

Moving forward, visualization helps to increase effectiveness. Consider this: What words would grace your resting place if you died today? Many of us are working efficiently but not effectively. We’re achieving goals that won’t matter in the end. There’s an old saying that when you don’t know where you are going, anywhere else becomes a destination.

To be efficient is to do much in so little time. Without a clear goal in mind, efficiency can become dangerous. You could be making progress but not in the right direction. Being effective means pursuing things that matter and understanding that everything else is a waste of time and effort. Productive people don’t just achieve random goals. They live their lives intentionally because they can see the big picture and invest their resources in the real deal. To define your goals and creed, write about your mission. It is a document that clarifies your principles and fundamental values. Your mission statement will guide your actions. However, writing a mission statement requires careful consideration; you shouldn’t do it in a hurry because it will form the foundation of your life.

Once you have that sense of mission, you have the essence of your own proactivity. You have the vision and the values which direct your life. ~ Stephen R. Covey

chapters.title: Schedule your priorities
chapters.text:
There are so many things that jostle for our attention. Your phone buzzes. Slack notifications pop up. Emails flood in. Your colleague stops by "just for a minute." Before you know it, your entire day has been hijacked by other people's priorities. chapters.title: Develop good relationships with others
chapters.text:
Goodwill, time, and effort are the currencies we spend in our interactions with people. The more of these we put in, the bigger our emotional deposit. Each time we hurt other people, we make withdrawals. A positive balance indicates a healthy relationship and vice versa.

The best strategy to solve potential problems is to prevent them. With no emotional deposit in a specific relationship, we need to mind our words and actions around people while taking steps to mend the connection.

Keeping your promises is a beacon of reliance, the highest currency in the emotional bank.

The practices to maintain A-class relationships are numerous:

1. Empathy and excellent listening skills: Conversely, things like selfishness, poor empathy, and broken promises are returnable through karma.
2. Personal integrity through loyalty and sensitivity to others’ needs: Gossiping is the opposite of this virtue. Being ready to defend someone in their absence is a sign of courtesy.
3. Making space for others: Engagement in peoples’ lives is a straightforward way to have healthy relationships.
4. Accountability: Those who recognize their mistakes and apologize have bigger deposits in the emotional bank.

How would you feel if the doctor handed you pills without listening to you properly? Indeed, you’d take their recommendations with suspicion. But many of us do it all the time with those around us. We hardly listen to understand; we listen to reply. We see the world through our own lens and assume others see it the same way. But they don't.

Therefore, empathy is one of the most valuable stocks we can invest in to make good deposits and positively impact others’ lives. Empathic listening requires us to immerse ourselves in the other person’s worldview. We need to feel what they feel and consider how they think. It’s like putting on someone else's glasses to see the world through their eyes.

When we practice empathic listening, people will open up to us more and take our advice because they’ll realize that we’re in sync with them. That’s why good listeners usually have healthy relationships.

Did you know? Communication experts say that body language and sounds take up 90% of our words.

chapters.title: Being respectful and open are essential ingredients for synergy
chapters.text:
When the sum is more than the parts combined, we have synergy. In other words, one plus one must be more than two. Differences in our worldviews and orientations make synergy difficult, if not impossible. But imagine if we are willing to admit our differences and recognize individual peculiarities. In that case, it may be possible to tap into the power of synergy. We don’t have to think the same way or have the same strengths and weaknesses. Think about a jazz ensemble. The drummer, guitarist, and saxophonist aren't playing the same notes — they're playing different parts that blend into something beautiful. That's synergy.

Synergy demands that we bring different things to the table, and we should value that.

All the previous habits build up the following one 一 taking responsibility, listening to others to understand them, valuing others' contribution, and applying it to solve a common problem and produce synergy.

David Lilienthal became the head of the Atomic Energy Commission after World War II. He gathered a team of the best minds in the country. Then, Lilienthal told them to take the first few weeks to get to know each other. He came under heavy criticism for doing this, but his goal was to create an atmosphere of trust. This exercise created a healthy work environment that produced excellent results.

All the group members felt respect toward each other and found solutions to disagreements. Having observed each other as personalities, their respect converted into understanding. As a result, the combined knowledge led to effective outcomes. This communication strategy is affordable and replicable in any group setting. The beauty is in respectful communication.

However, respectful communication doesn’t involve excessive emotions to the point of empathy. Here, recognition of others’ skills and knowledge is sufficient for productive results. Emotions, on the other hand, can cause personal confrontations. Either way, reliance is the key element of mature collaboration.

chapters.title: Take time out to sharpen your physical, spiritual, mental, and social life
chapters.text:
We are the most important asset ever available in our lives. We are the only promise to ourselves and are free to respond and change to external circumstances. As long as we stay sensitive to everything around us, this asset will grow in value. Investment in ourselves is the only deposit that brings profit regardless of economic or social situation. To achieve this, we must care for ourselves physically, mentally, and spiritually.

To sustain our effectiveness, we must take time to recharge our batteries. It’s like sharpening a saw. When loggers cut down trees, they take time to sharpen the saw to stay effective; this is never time wasted and is a worthwhile investment. The logger who doesn’t stop to sharpen their saw won’t cut down more trees than the one who does.

There are four dimensions to our lives as humans we need to keep sharp: physical, spiritual, mental, and social (emotional).

• Physically, we need to be mindful of what we eat, exercise regularly, and only allow a healthy dose of stress. Exercising feels more time-affordable when viewing it as a 30-minutes session per day.
• Spiritually, mindfulness exercises, prayer, and reflecting on our values and principles are essential practices. The practice differs for everyone, but the final goal is achieving harmony within oneself.
• Mentally, we need to engage in our hobbies more and read good books. Cut down the time spent watching TV and create something worthwhile. Journaling and writing poems or letters are some examples.
• Socially, we can make efforts to build healthy relationships. Participate in activities that involve other people and show genuine love to them. Make attempts to live peacefully with everyone.

At the same time, we should not isolate ourselves from others. We receive influence from others and affect them mutually. In effect, we serve as social mirrors for each other. The more positive the reflection is, the greater the chance of meeting proactive individuals and adopting their vision.

Take time to reflect and renew your strength. Evaluating your performance helps you see what you need to work on.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
At first, humans are complicated. However, diving deep into our psyche, there are simple keys to unlocking our potential. Stephen R. Covey observed them as parts of the human condition and offered seven essential habits to improve people’s lives significantly:

1. Being proactive
2. Starting with the end in mind
3. Scheduling your priorities
4. Thinking win-win
5. Prioritizing understanding over being understood
6. Synergizing
7. Sharpening the saw

How about today? Can we make use of these habits in the 21st century? For sure, if we want to pursue being highly effective in our lives, jobs, and relationships. Cultivating values, showing empathy for others, and not dispersing our energy over insignificant matters will make life more wholesome.

However, the new era also requires new habits like resilience, flexibility, and critical thinking. We must learn to tolerate circumstances, perceive them as the natural way of things, and be willing to take risks. Our environments are changing. The good news is that today, with advanced technology, we can observe these changes directly and with more scrutiny.

Perseverance, adaptability, and brainstorming may be a starter subset for the highly efficient person of the 21st century.

Try this:

• Show up when people need you: Sometimes just being there means great support.
• Always say “thank you” to recognize someone’s efforts: Especially someone unconfident.
• See them: Here, consider being an empathetic listener who asks questions about what bothers the other person rather than talking excessively about your experiences.
• Choose one habit to master: Don't try to implement all seven habits at once. Pick the one that resonates most strongly and focus on it for the next 30 days until it becomes natural.

Remember: your most effective self is within reach, just seven habits away.
